topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the screen, the numbers swimming before his eyes. The quarterly report, usually a source of professional satisfaction, had instead delivered a sharp, unwelcome jolt of reality. Sarah, the bright-eyed intern who always seemed to be on the verge of a breakthrough, had just received an unexpected bonus that dwarfed his own annual raise. A slow, cold dread began to settle in his chest, heavy and suffocating. He traced the edge of his desk with a trembling finger, the polished wood feeling alien beneath his touch. He wanted to look away, to pretend the figures didn't exist, but his gaze kept snagging on the discrepancy. When Sarah approached him later, offering a cheerful, oblivious greeting, Elias managed a tight, strained smile that didn't reach his eyes. "That's... good news for you, Sarah," he mumbled, the words tasting like ash. He felt a strange tightness behind his ribs, a physical constriction that made taking a full breath feel like a deliberate effort.

[story 2]

“Seriously?” Mark whispered, the sound barely audible over the hum of the office air conditioning. He reread the pay stubs three times, his jaw clenching so hard his molars ached. Chloe, the newest hire, had somehow landed a position with a salary that was nearly double his own, a fact that felt like a physical blow to his confidence. He leaned back in his chair, the leather groaning in protest, and let out a long, shaky exhale. His hands, resting on the keyboard, began to shake almost imperceptibly. He looked around the open-plan office, the cheerful chatter of his colleagues sounding distant and mocking. He felt a profound sense of deflation, as if the floor had suddenly dropped out from under him. When Chloe walked by, offering a casual wave, Mark simply nodded, his expression carefully neutral, while internally, he cataloged every slight shift in her posture, every casual gesture, as if searching for some hidden justification for the imbalance.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the cubicle farm seemed to buzz with aggressive intensity. David picked up his coffee mug, but his fingers were clumsy, causing the ceramic to rattle against the metal coaster. He had seen the data, and the stark reality of the pay disparity with his junior colleague, Ben, had settled over him like a heavy, damp blanket. A profound emptiness bloomed in his stomach, a hollow ache that made his shoulders slump involuntarily. He found himself avoiding eye contact with everyone, retreating into the periphery of his workspace. When Ben stopped by to ask a quick question about a project, David managed a strained, overly bright tone. "Everything's running smoothly, Ben. Really smooth." The effort to sound confident was exhausting; his voice came out thin and reedy, and he could feel a distinct warmth spreading across his cheeks, quickly followed by a chilling numbness.
